questions.promptTex: \text{Which of these is a prime number?}
hints: A prime has only $1$ and itself as factors.
questions.explanation: $23$ is prime — its only factors are $1$ and $23$. The others all factor: $15 = 3 \cdot 5$, $21 = 3 \cdot 7$, $27 = 3^3$.
steps: $23$: try dividing by $2, 3$. None work, and $\sqrt{23} < 5$, so we're done.

questions.promptTex: \text{How many factors does } 24 \text{ have?}
questions.explanation: Pair the factors: $1 \cdot 24$, $2 \cdot 12$, $3 \cdot 8$, $4 \cdot 6$ — four pairs, $8$ factors total: $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24\}$.
steps: $5$ doesn't divide $24$ — and the next would be $6 \times 4$, which we already have.

questions.promptTex: \text{Which number is divisible by } 9?
hints: Divisibility rule for $9$: digit sum must be divisible by $9$.
steps: Apply the rule: a number is divisible by $9$ if and only if its digit sum is.

questions.explanation: Common factors of $12$ and $18$: $\{1, 2, 3, 6\}$. Greatest is $6$. (Or: $12 = 2^2 \cdot 3$, $18 = 2 \cdot 3^2$ — take min of each exponent: $2^1 \cdot 3^1 = 6$.)

questions.promptTex: \mathrm{lcm}(4, 6) = ?
questions.explanation: Multiples of $4$: $4, 8, 12, \ldots$. Multiples of $6$: $6, 12, 18, \ldots$. Smallest common: $12$. (Or: $4 = 2^2$, $6 = 2 \cdot 3$ — take max of each exponent: $2^2 \cdot 3 = 12$.)

questions.promptTex: \text{Which is the prime factorization of } 60?
hints: Prime factorization means EVERY factor must be prime.
$30, 15, 4$ are not prime — keep splitting until they are.
questions.explanation: $60 = 2 \cdot 30 = 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 15 = 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 = 2^2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5$. Every factor in the answer must be prime, so 'all primes' is the requirement that rules out the other choices.
$48 = 2^4 \cdot 3$ and $36 = 2^2 \cdot 3^2$. Min exponents: $2^2 \cdot 3^1 = 4 \cdot 3 = 12$.
steps: For each prime, take the SMALLER exponent: $\min(4, 2) = 2$ for $2$, $\min(1, 2) = 1$ for $3$.

questions.promptTex: \mathrm{lcm}(8, 12) = ?
steps: Take MAX exponent for each prime: $\max(3, 2) = 3$ for $2$, $\max(0, 1) = 1$ for $3$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two clocks chime every } 12 \text{ and } 18 \text{ minutes.} \\ \text{They just chimed together. After how many minutes will they chime together again?}
hints: Both chime together at every common multiple — find the smallest one.
questions.explanation: Both chime at every multiple of LCM. $12 = 2^2 \cdot 3$, $18 = 2 \cdot 3^2$, so $\mathrm{lcm}(12, 18) = 2^2 \cdot 3^2 = 36$. Next sync: $36$ minutes.
steps: They sync at every common multiple — smallest is $\mathrm{lcm}$.

questions.promptTex: \text{What is the largest square tile (in inches) that fits exactly} \\ \text{(no cutting) into a } 24 \times 36 \text{ inch floor?}
hints: The tile side must divide BOTH dimensions evenly. Largest such number?
questions.explanation: Tile side must divide both $24$ and $36$. The biggest such number is $\gcd(24, 36) = 12$. So a $12 \times 12$ tile works perfectly: $2$ tiles wide, $3$ tiles tall.
steps: Tile side $s$ must satisfy $s \mid 24$ AND $s \mid 36$ (divides both).
$12$-inch tiles: floor is $2$ tiles wide, $3$ tiles tall — fits exactly.

questions.promptTex: \text{Use the identity } \gcd(a, b) \cdot \mathrm{lcm}(a, b) = a \cdot b. \\ \text{If } \gcd(15, 20) = 5, \text{ what is } \mathrm{lcm}(15, 20)?
\text{Find the smallest number greater than } 1 \text{ that is divisible by} \\ \text{each of } 2, 3, 4, 5, 6.
hints: Prime-factorize each, then take max exponent of each prime.
questions.explanation: Need $\mathrm{lcm}(2, 3, 4, 5, 6)$. Primes: $2 = 2$, $3 = 3$, $4 = 2^2$, $5 = 5$, $6 = 2 \cdot 3$. Max exponents: $2^2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 = 60$. ($\mathrm{lcm}$ for more than two numbers works the same way.)
steps: For each prime, max exponent: $2^2$ (from $4$), $3$ (from $3$ or $6$), $5$ (from $5$).

questions.promptTex: \text{How many factors does } 36 \text{ have?}
hints: Pair factors. $36$ is special — one of the factors pairs with itself.
questions.explanation: $36 = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 9, 12, 18, 36\}$ — that's $9$ factors. Note: $6 \cdot 6 = 36$, so $6$ pairs with itself — this is why all perfect squares have an ODD number of factors.
steps: Total: $4$ regular pairs ($8$ factors) + $1$ self-pair ($1$ factor) = $9$.
Perfect-squares always have odd factor counts because of the self-pair.

questions.promptTex: \text{Find } \gcd(84, 126).
questions.explanation: $84 = 2^2 \cdot 3 \cdot 7$ and $126 = 2 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 7$. Min exponents: $2^1 \cdot 3^1 \cdot 7^1 = 42$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Find } \mathrm{lcm}(15, 20, 25).
hints: For each prime, take MAX exponent.
questions.explanation: $15 = 3 \cdot 5$, $20 = 2^2 \cdot 5$, $25 = 5^2$. Max exponents: $2^2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5^2 = 4 \cdot 3 \cdot 25 = 300$.
steps: For each prime: $2$ appears as $2^2$ (max), $3$ as $3$ (max), $5$ as $5^2$ (max).

questions.promptTex: \text{A teacher has } 24 \text{ pencils and } 36 \text{ erasers and wants to make identical} \\ \text{goodie bags using ALL the items. What is the largest possible number of bags?}
hints: The number of bags must divide both 24 AND 36.
questions.explanation: Each bag must have the same number of pencils and erasers, so the number of bags must divide both $24$ and $36$. Largest such number is $\gcd(24, 36) = 12$. (Each bag gets $24/12 = 2$ pencils and $36/12 = 3$ erasers.)
steps: Number of bags $b$ must divide $24$ (pencils split equally) AND $36$ (erasers).

questions.promptTex: \text{Which of these is NOT a prime number?}
hints: A prime has only 1 and itself as factors.
questions.explanation: $21 = 3 \cdot 7$, so it has factors other than $1$ and itself. The other three are prime.
steps: Check each: $17, 19, 23$ — try dividing by $2, 3$. None work and the squares pass them, so they're prime.

questions.promptTex: \text{Which number is divisible by } 3?
hints: Divisibility rule for 3: digit sum must be divisible by 3.

questions.promptTex: \text{Which number is divisible by } 4?
hints: Divisibility rule for 4: the last TWO digits must form a number divisible by 4.
questions.explanation: Look at the last two digits: $30, 46, 32, 10$. Of these, only $32$ is divisible by $4$. So $332$ is divisible by $4$. (Check: $332 / 4 = 83$ ✓.)
steps: Apply the rule: a number is divisible by $4$ iff its last two digits are.

questions.promptTex: \text{How many factors does } 18 \text{ have?}
questions.explanation: Factors: $\{1, 2, 3, 6, 9, 18\}$ — $6$ in total. (Pair them: $1 \cdot 18$, $2 \cdot 9$, $3 \cdot 6$.)
steps: $1 \times 18$, $2 \times 9$, $3 \times 6$ — and we've met in the middle.

questions.explanation: Common factors of $8$ and $12$: $\{1, 2, 4\}$. Greatest is $4$.

questions.promptTex: \mathrm{lcm}(3, 5) = ?
questions.explanation: $3$ and $5$ are coprime (share no factors > 1), so $\mathrm{lcm} = 3 \cdot 5 = 15$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Which is the prime factorization of } 50?
hints: Every factor must be PRIME. $25$ and $10$ aren't prime.
questions.explanation: $50 = 2 \cdot 25 = 2 \cdot 5 \cdot 5 = 2 \cdot 5^2$. The other choices either include composites ($25$, $10$) or have wrong powers ($2^2 \cdot 5 = 20$, not $50$).

questions.promptTex: \text{How many factors does } 100 \text{ have?}
hints: $100 = 10 \times 10$, so $10$ pairs with itself — perfect square trick.
questions.explanation: Factors: $\{1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, 25, 50, 100\}$ — $9$ in total. Pairs: $1 \times 100$, $2 \times 50$, $4 \times 25$, $5 \times 20$, $10 \times 10$ (self-pair → odd count).
steps: $10$ pairs with itself, so total = $4$ pairs $\times 2$ + $1$ self = $9$.

questions.promptTex: \text{What is the smallest prime number greater than } 30?
hints: Test $31, 32, 33, \ldots$ until you find a prime.
$31$: not divisible by $2, 3$ (digit sum $4$), $5$ — and $\sqrt{31} < 6$, so we're done.
questions.explanation: $31$: not divisible by $2$ (odd), $3$ (digit sum $4$), or $5$ (doesn't end in $0$/$5$). $\sqrt{31} \approx 5.57$, so no need to test $7$ or higher. $31$ is prime.
steps: Test small primes up to $\sqrt{31} \approx 5.57$: try $2, 3, 5$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Which is the prime factorization of } 72?
questions.explanation: $72 = 8 \cdot 9 = 2^3 \cdot 3^2$. ($8 \cdot 9$ is correct numerically but isn't a PRIME factorization since neither is prime.) Check: $2^2 \cdot 3^3 = 4 \cdot 27 = 108$, not $72$.
$60 = 2^2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5$, $90 = 2 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 5$. Min exponents: $2^1 \cdot 3^1 \cdot 5^1 = 30$.
steps: Take min exponent of each prime: $\min(2,1)=1$, $\min(1,2)=1$, $\min(1,1)=1$.

questions.promptTex: \mathrm{lcm}(20, 30) = ?
questions.explanation: $20 = 2^2 \cdot 5$, $30 = 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5$. Max exponents: $2^2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 = 60$.
steps: Max exponent of each prime: $2$ (from $20$), $1$ (from $30$), $1$ (from either).

questions.promptTex: \text{A teacher has } 30 \text{ cookies and } 45 \text{ apples to put into identical goodie bags,} \\ \text{using ALL items. What's the largest number of bags she can make?}
hints: Each bag must have the same count of cookies AND apples — so the number of bags must divide both $30$ and $45$.
questions.explanation: Number of bags must divide both $30$ and $45$. Largest such number is $\gcd(30, 45) = 15$. Each bag gets $30/15 = 2$ cookies and $45/15 = 3$ apples.
steps: Constraint: bag count $b$ must divide both $30$ and $45$ exactly.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two traffic lights cycle every } 30 \text{ seconds and } 45 \text{ seconds.} \\ \text{They just turned green together. After how many seconds do they next turn green together?}
hints: They sync at every common multiple. Find the smallest one.
questions.explanation: $30 = 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5$, $45 = 3^2 \cdot 5$. $\mathrm{lcm} = 2 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 5 = 90$ seconds (1.5 minutes).
steps: Lights sync at every common multiple of their cycles.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two ropes have lengths } 36 \text{ m and } 24 \text{ m.} \\ \text{You want to cut both into pieces of equal length, with no waste,} \\ \text{using the LONGEST possible piece length. How long is each piece (in meters)?}
hints: Piece length must divide BOTH $36$ and $24$ evenly.
questions.explanation: $\gcd(36, 24) = 12$. Each piece is $12$ m. (Check: $36 / 12 = 3$ pieces from one rope, $24 / 12 = 2$ from the other — no waste.)

questions.promptTex: \text{Two runners go around a track. Runner A finishes a lap every } 4 \text{ minutes,} \\ \text{Runner B every } 6. \text{ If they start together, how many minutes until they meet at the start again?}
hints: They meet at the start every common multiple. Find the smallest.
questions.explanation: $\mathrm{lcm}(4, 6) = 12$. After $12$ minutes: Runner A has done $3$ laps, B has done $2$ — both back at the start.
steps: They both arrive at the start at every common multiple of $4$ and $6$ minutes.
Verification: A does $12/4 = 3$ laps, B does $12/6 = 2$ — both finish at the start.

questions.promptTex: \text{What is the LCD (least common denominator) for } \dfrac{1}{4} + \dfrac{1}{6} + \dfrac{1}{8}?
hints: LCD = LCM of all the denominators.
questions.explanation: $\mathrm{lcm}(4, 6, 8)$: max exponents → $2^3 \cdot 3 = 24$. So the LCD is $24$. (Then $\dfrac{1}{4} + \dfrac{1}{6} + \dfrac{1}{8} = \dfrac{6 + 4 + 3}{24} = \dfrac{13}{24}$.)

questions.promptTex: \text{How many factors does } 72 \text{ have?}
hints: Use the prime factorization shortcut: if $n = p^a \cdot q^b$, then number of factors is $(a+1)(b+1)$.
steps: Apply the formula: number of factors = $(3+1)(2+1) = 4 \cdot 3 = 12$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two printers take } 6 \text{ and } 8 \text{ minutes to print a poster.} \\ \text{They both start at minute } 0. \text{ When do they finish posters at the same minute next?}
hints: Both finish a poster at every common multiple of their cycle times.
questions.explanation: $6 = 2 \cdot 3$, $8 = 2^3$. $\mathrm{lcm} = 2^3 \cdot 3 = 24$ minutes. (At minute $24$: printer 1 has done $4$ posters, printer 2 has done $3$.)
steps: Each printer finishes at every multiple of its time.
Both finish together at every common multiple — smallest is $\mathrm{lcm}$.

questions.promptTex: \text{A rectangle has area } 36 \text{ square units (whole-number sides).} \\ \text{How many DIFFERENT dimensions } (l \times w) \text{ are possible, treating } l \times w \text{ and } w \times l \text{ as the same?}
hints: Each dimension pair is $l \times w$ where $l \cdot w = 36$ — i.e., a factor pair of $36$.
questions.explanation: Factor pairs of $36$ (unordered): $1 \times 36$, $2 \times 18$, $3 \times 12$, $4 \times 9$, $6 \times 6$. That's $5$ different rectangles. (The $6 \times 6$ is a square.)
steps: Need pairs $(l, w)$ with $l \cdot w = 36$ and $l \leq w$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Class A has } 18 \text{ students; Class B has } 24. \text{ They form groups for a project,} \\ \text{each group with the same number of A students AND the same number of B students.} \\ \text{What is the LARGEST number of groups possible?}
questions.explanation: $\gcd(18, 24) = 6$ groups. Each group has $18/6 = 3$ A-students and $24/6 = 4$ B-students.
steps: Number of groups $g$ must divide both $18$ and $24$.
Each group: $3$ from A, $4$ from B.

questions.promptTex: \text{A garden has } 24 \text{ plants. A gardener wants to plant them in equal-length rows.} \\ \text{How many DIFFERENT row-lengths are possible (counting } 1 \text{ row of } 24 \text{ and } 24 \text{ rows of } 1)?
hints: Each valid row-length is a factor of $24$.
questions.explanation: Row-length must divide $24$. Factors: $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24\}$ — $8$ choices.

questions.promptTex: \text{Hot dogs come in packs of } 10, \text{ buns in packs of } 8. \\ \text{What is the SMALLEST number of each you must buy to have} \\ \text{equal numbers of dogs and buns?}
hints: You need a number that's both a multiple of $10$ AND of $8$.
questions.explanation: $\mathrm{lcm}(10, 8)$: $10 = 2 \cdot 5$, $8 = 2^3$, max exponents: $2^3 \cdot 5 = 40$. Buy $40$ of each: $4$ packs of dogs, $5$ packs of buns.
steps: Need $N$ such that $10 \mid N$ and $8 \mid N$.
$4$ packs of dogs ($4 \cdot 10 = 40$), $5$ packs of buns ($5 \cdot 8 = 40$).

questions.promptTex: \text{One clock chimes every } 60 \text{ minutes; another every } 90 \text{ minutes.} \\ \text{Both just chimed. How many MINUTES until they chime together again?}
hints: Both chime at every common multiple of $60$ and $90$.
questions.explanation: $60 = 2^2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5$, $90 = 2 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 5$. $\mathrm{lcm} = 2^2 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 5 = 180$ minutes (3 hours).
steps: Sync at every common multiple of $60$ and $90$.

questions.promptTex: \text{A baker has icing tubes of length } 60, 84, \text{ and } 96 \text{ cm.} \\ \text{She wants to cut all of them into pieces of the same length, with no waste,} \\ \text{using the LONGEST possible piece. What length (cm)?}
hints: The piece length must divide ALL three lengths.
questions.explanation: $60 = 2^2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5$, $84 = 2^2 \cdot 3 \cdot 7$, $96 = 2^5 \cdot 3$. Min exponents: $2^2 \cdot 3 = 12$ cm. (Pieces: $5 + 7 + 8 = 20$ pieces total.)
steps: Min exponents (only $2$ and $3$ appear in all): $2^2 \cdot 3 = 12$ cm.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two numbers have } \gcd = 4 \text{ and } \mathrm{lcm} = 24. \\ \text{What is their PRODUCT?}
hints: Use the identity $\gcd(a, b) \cdot \mathrm{lcm}(a, b) = a \cdot b$.
questions.explanation: By the identity, $a \cdot b = \gcd \cdot \mathrm{lcm} = 4 \cdot 24 = 96$. (No need to find $a$ and $b$ themselves.)

hints: For three numbers: prime-factorize each, then take the MIN exponent of each prime.
questions.explanation: $36 = 2^2 \cdot 3^2$, $60 = 2^2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5$, $84 = 2^2 \cdot 3 \cdot 7$. Min exponents: $2^2 \cdot 3 = 12$. (Primes $5$ and $7$ aren't in all three, so they're excluded from GCD.)
steps: GCD uses only primes in ALL three (here: $2$ and $3$).

questions.promptTex: \mathrm{lcm}(6, 8, 10) = ?
hints: Prime-factorize each, take MAX exponent of each prime.
questions.explanation: $6 = 2 \cdot 3$, $8 = 2^3$, $10 = 2 \cdot 5$. Max exponents: $2^3 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 = 120$.
steps: Take max exponent of each prime: $2^3$ (from $8$), $3^1$ (from $6$), $5^1$ (from $10$).

questions.promptTex: \text{Three lights flash every } 12, 18, \text{ and } 30 \text{ seconds.} \\ \text{They just all flashed together. How many SECONDS until all three flash together again?}
hints: All flash together at every common multiple of $12, 18, 30$ — smallest is the LCM.
questions.explanation: $12 = 2^2 \cdot 3$, $18 = 2 \cdot 3^2$, $30 = 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5$. Max exponents: $2^2 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 5 = 180$ seconds = $3$ minutes.
steps: All sync at every common multiple — smallest is $\mathrm{lcm}$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Train A leaves every } 36 \text{ minutes; Train B every } 60. \\ \text{Both leave at } 8\text{:}00 \text{ AM. When do they next leave together?}
hints: Both leave together at every common multiple of $36$ and $60$ minutes — smallest is the LCM.
questions.explanation: $36 = 2^2 \cdot 3^2$, $60 = 2^2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5$. $\mathrm{lcm} = 2^2 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 5 = 180$ min = $3$ hours. $8\text{:}00$ AM + $3$ hours = $11\text{:}00$ AM.
steps: Both leave together at every $\mathrm{lcm}(36, 60)$ minutes.

questions.promptTex: \text{Three drummers strike on different beats: } A \text{ every } 4, B \text{ every } 6, C \text{ every } 9. \\ \text{They all strike on beat } 0. \text{ How many BEATS until all three strike together again?}
hints: All strike together at every common multiple of $4, 6, 9$.
steps: Sync at every common multiple — smallest is LCM.
All three drum together every $36$ beats.

questions.promptTex: \text{How many factors does } 360 \text{ have?}
hints: Number of factors $= (a+1)(b+1)(c+1)$ from the exponents.
questions.explanation: $360 = 2^3 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 5$. Number of factors $= (3+1)(2+1)(1+1) = 4 \cdot 3 \cdot 2 = 24$.
steps: (So $360$ — used as the number of degrees in a circle precisely because of its many factors!)
